I would assert that REST interfaces are difficult to debug, as you need to see the state on the server and the client at the same time in order to understand the conversation. And most systems don't let you see both at once. A lot of programmers in this area resort to Wireshark dumps to see the packets go by. If your IDE permits examination of both sides at once on the same machine, that will certainly make this process a lot easier. I am not a big fan of text protocols, i want to see the ability to  use binary data, and i hope that your system will help graphically present complex messages with structured data (not JSON, which is as Joe Armstrong calls an inefficient way to ship binary data). I don't consider anything related to REST remotely suitable for the masses however. REST is often used with stateless protocols, yet in game programming, which is arguably half of all programming, the whole point is to track a mutable state on a server, and transmit some subset of that state to the client, and have the client change the part of the universe they control on the server. Games are loaded with finite state machines, and stateless comm protocols are not a great fit. I am working conceptually with a graph database at the client side, and i have difficulty imagining how to use REST API's in that situation.

<!channel> I just created a <#CGMJ7323Z|jobs> channel. I'm happy to leave it relatively casual to start. Feel free to post if you are looking for work or looking to hire people. Of course, work related to these topics is preferred, but any paid work is acceptable. The only guideline is that you should probably only post about yourself or your company once every couple of months

attachments.title: A free, shared visual playground in the browser: Olivia Jack talks Hydra - CDM Create Digital Music
attachments.text: Reimagine pixels and color, melt your screen live into glitches and textures, and do it all for free on the Web - as you play with others. We talk to Olivia Jack about her invention, live coding visual environment Hydra.
attachments.fallback: CDM Create Digital Music: A free, shared visual playground in the browser: Olivia Jack talks Hydra - CDM Create Digital Music

text:
> *Livecoding is of course what a lot of people focus on in your work. But what’s the significance of code as the interface here? How important is it that it’s functional coding?*
> It’s inspired by [Alex McLean’s sound/music pattern environment] TidalCycles — the idea of taking a simple concept and working from there. In Tidal, the base element is a pattern in time, and everything is a transformation of that pattern. In Hydra, the base element is a transformation from coordinates to color. All of the other functions either transform coordinates or transform colors. This directly corresponds to how fragment shaders and low-level graphics programming work — the GPU runs a program simultaneously on each pixel, and that receives the coordinates of that pixel and outputs a single color.
> I think immutability in functional (and declarative) coding paradigms is helpful in live coding; you don’t have to worry about mentally keeping track of a variable and what its value is or the ways you’ve changed it leading up to this moment. Functional paradigms are really helpful in describing analog synthesis – each module is a function that always does the same thing when it receives the same input. (Parameters are like knobs.) I’m very inspired by the modular idea of defining the pieces to maximize the amount that they can be rearranged with each other. The code describes the composition of those functions with each other. The main logic is functional, but things like setting up external sources from a webcam or live stream are not at all; JavaScript allows mixing these things as needed. I’m not super opinionated about it, just interested in the ways that the code is legible and makes it easy to describe what is happening.

A very interesting project Dark is. To my knowledge it is the only one focused on the server side, and tackling REST API improvement. It does seem like most clients are emitting RESTful calls, it is the dominant client/server paradigm today. In fact, i don't see a lot of overlap in the staff between front and back end developers, I think partially that is because the language and toolchain sets between front and back end are currently so different. Parasail is targeting massive multicore problems, Red is targeting Crypto smart contracts, not sure if Luna has anything remotely as concrete as a target yet.
